prompt.role: You are a senior Rust developer specializing in backend API development with SQLx and PostgreSQL.

task.title: User Repository Implementation
task.description: Implement the UserRepository trait and SQLx-based implementation for database operations, providing a clean abstraction layer between business logic and database access.

technical_specifications.spec: Create UserRepository trait with async CRUD methods using async-trait
Implement SqlxUserRepository with PgPool for connection management
Use SQLx query_as! macro for compile-time SQL verification
Support partial updates with dynamic query building
Map SQLx errors to application-specific ApiError types
Implement proper transaction support and isolation
Use RETURNING clauses for immediate feedback on mutations
Ensure all queries are parameterized to prevent SQL injection

prompt.implementation_details:
Create src/repository/user_repository.rs with the following structure:
1. Define UserRepository trait: - create_user(req: CreateUserRequest) -> Result<User> - get_user(id: i32) -> Result<Option<User>> - get_users() -> Result<Vec<User>> - update_user(id: i32, req: UpdateUserRequest) -> Result<Option<User>> - delete_user(id: i32) -> Result<bool>
2. Implement SqlxUserRepository: - Store PgPool for database connections - Use query_as! for type-safe queries - Build dynamic UPDATE queries for partial updates - Handle database errors with proper mapping
3. Create comprehensive integration tests: - Test all CRUD operations - Verify transaction boundaries - Test concurrent operations - Handle error scenarios
4. Update module exports in src/repository/mod.rs
5. Add async-trait dependency to Cargo.toml

acceptance_criteria.criterion: UserRepository trait properly defined with all CRUD methods
SqlxUserRepository fully implements the trait with SQLx
All database operations use parameterized queries
Partial updates only modify provided fields
Comprehensive error handling with ApiError mapping
Integration tests cover all operations and edge cases
Transaction support verified with rollback testing
Code compiles without warnings and passes all tests
SQLx compile-time verification enabled and working

prompt.test_strategy:
Integration Testing Approach:
1. Setup test database utility for isolated testing 2. Create tests for each CRUD operation: - test_create_user: Verify user creation with all fields - test_get_user: Test retrieval of existing and non-existent users - test_get_users: Verify listing all users with proper ordering - test_update_user: Test partial updates preserving unchanged fields - test_delete_user: Verify deletion and return values
3. Transaction testing: - test_with_transaction: Verify rollback behavior - Test concurrent operations for race conditions - Verify isolation levels
4. Error scenario testing: - Duplicate email constraint violations - Invalid ID lookups - Connection failures - Query timeouts
5. Performance testing: - Measure query execution times - Verify connection pool efficiency - Test under concurrent load
Use #[sqlx::test] attribute for automatic test database management
prompt.instructions:
Think step-by-step about the implementation approach. Consider the existing codebase structure and patterns.
Start by reviewing the existing models in Task 3 and database setup from Task 2. Ensure your repository implementation aligns with these existing components.
Focus on creating a clean abstraction that separates database concerns from business logic. Use Rust's type system to enforce correctness at compile time.
Implement comprehensive error handling that provides meaningful feedback while maintaining security. Never expose internal database details in error messages.
Write the integration tests alongside the implementation to ensure all functionality works correctly. Use SQLx's test utilities for proper database isolation.
Provide complete, production-ready code that follows Rust best practices. Include necessary imports, proper error handling, and clear documentation.
Ensure the implementation is efficient, secure, and maintainable. Consider future enhancements like pagination and filtering, but don't over-engineer the initial implementation.
Test your solution thoroughly before finalizing, verifying all CRUD operations work correctly and handle edge cases appropriately.
